data.title: Ginkgo Bioworks: Shedding Biosecurity And Doubling Down On Autonomy
data.description:
Ginkgo Bioworks (DNA) reported soft results again in the fourth quarter of 2025, and made the decision to divest its biosecurity business. While cost-cutting efforts have reduced cash burn, breakeven is unlikely without significant growth, and Ginkgo's balance sheet continues to weaken.

I previously suggested that while Ginkgo was facing a weak demand environment, its biggest problem was a failure to find product-market fit. It is therefore not surprising that Ginkgo is enacting another strategic pivot to try and reinvigorate its business. The latest move is an attempt to capitalize on the emerging AI-enabled drug discovery opportunity and appears more likely to succeed. While Ginkgo is well positioned to capitalize on AI related demand, I remain neutral on the company's prospects until either the macro environment improves or Ginkgo's latest initiative demonstrates meaningful traction.

Market Conditions

Ginkgo continues to face a challenging demand environment, even if biopharma stocks rebounded somewhat in 2025. Funding for academic customers has been under pressure, and elevated interest rates generally remain an issue. While the impact of this has been fairly broad-based, Ginkgo's industrial and consumer businesses have been particularly hard hit.

There appears to be an emerging opportunity for companies that are able to support data generation for AI-enabled drug discovery though. For example, close to 40% of Twist Bioscience's (TWST) order growth in FY2025 was related to AI drug discovery, with customers including big tech companies like Google (GOOG).

Isomorphic Labs is a good illustration of the AI-enabled drug discovery opportunity. The company is focused on structure-based design using AI protein structure and binding-affinity prediction models. AlphaFold has been around for some time, but Isomorphic recently took another step forward with its Drug Design Engine, which improves generalizability and binding-affinity prediction. While Isomorphic Labs is focused on in-silico drug discovery, it still needs data to train and validate its models.

Ginkgo Bioworks Business Updates

Gingko's Cell Engineering business has shifted its focus towards projects that are smaller in scope in recent years, driven by its automation, AI, and Datapoints products. The results have been disappointing so far though, and Ginkgo is doubling down on autonomous labs in response. The company wants to demonstrate the capabilities of autonomous labs by moving its own R&D services into a single autonomous facility. Ginkgo will decommission its lab benches and replace them with work cells (100 RACs in the first half of 2026).

Customers can either utilize Ginkgo's in-house autonomous labs or purchase RACs directly. Ginkgo wants to drive adoption amongst large pharma companies, and hardware sales appear to be a focus of these efforts. Ginkgo already has a 47 million USD deal with Pacific Northwest National Labs (DoE) which will involve the installation of 97 RACs in an autonomous lab.

The company also wants to enable open-ended autonomous research through the use of AI. The company recently demonstrated this in collaboration with OpenAI. GPT-5 was used to design experiments, before submitting them to Ginkgo's autonomous lab in Boston. The lab would then send the results to GPT-5 and the model would iterate. This process resulted in the cost of cell-free protein synthesis being lowered 40%.

Ginkgo's Cell Engineering business had 109 revenue-generating programs at the end of 2025, a decline of 4% YoY. While Ginkgo has suggested that the drop is due to program rationalization as part of its restructuring efforts, it is hard not to feel that demand for the company's services remains weak. The Datapoints business managed to land 10 top pharma customers in its first full year though.

Gingko is also spinning off its biosecurity business, which will be taken private and operated independently. In return, Ginkgo will receive a 20% stake in the new company, Tower Biosecurity. While biosecurity has only ever been a small part of the Gingko story, it has been the primary driver of the company's financial success. The terms of the deal seem to indicate that Ginkgo no longer views this business as a priority and is primarily focused on eliminating biosecurity costs.

Financial Analysis

Ginkgo's Cell Engineering revenue totalled 26 million USD in the fourth quarter of 2025, down 26% YoY. Biosecurity revenue was only 7 million USD, although this is no longer much of a consideration.

Ginkgo hasn't provided revenue guidance for 2026, adding to investor uncertainty. Given the Biosecurity divestment and the current run-rate of the Cell Engineering business, it would not be surprising if revenue is down something like 25% YoY in 2026 though.

Ginkgo's Cell Engineering segment loss was 17 million USD in the fourth quarter. Cash burn dropped from 383 million USD in 2024 to 171 million USD in 2025, and totaled 47 million USD in the fourth quarter.

Cash burn is expected to further decline to 125-150 million USD in 2026. The shift towards autonomous labs will shrink Ginkgo's footprint, helping the company to reduce costs. The benefit of this will be somewhat muted while the commercial real estate market prevents Ginkgo from unloading much of this cost though. The carrying cost of excess lease space was 54 million USD in 2025 and Ginkgo has been fighting to reduce this for some time.

Cash burn isn't overly concerning given that Ginkgo retains over 423 million USD in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities. If the company can't start to generate meaningful growth over the next 1-2 years, its balance sheet is going to become a cause for investor concern though.

Conclusion

Ginkgo Bioworks has roughly a 400 million USD enterprise value versus something like a 100 million USD Cell Engineering revenue run rate. The company expects around 150 million USD cash burn in 2026 though, meaning its forward EV/S ratio is probably still around 5.5. This is still high for an unprofitable company that is yet to demonstrate consistent growth from its core business.

Ginkgo has now enacted 2 major strategic pivots to try and correct this. While move away from owning IP and introducing new products and services has had little impact so far, automation appears to be a more compelling value proposition. There is growing demand for data in support of AI at the moment, and Ginkgo is well positioned to capitalize on this. I remain neutral to negative on Ginkgo's prospects though, as growth is likely to disappoint in the near-term and losses are likely to remain elevated.

data.title: Moody's Corporation: Cheap Enough And Well-Protected
data.description:
It has been a little more than two years since I published my last article about Moody’s Corporation (MCO). While I always acknowledged that Moody’s Corporation was (and still is) a high-quality business, I also considered the stock price to always be a little too high over the last few years. In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

I have been wrong in my last article and Moody's was once again able to reach its previous all-time high (set in late 2021). And now we saw the stock price bounce off that high once again - and in theory we could see a double-top with the risk of steeply declining stock prices.

There is one major risk I see - a long-lasting process of deleveraging especially in the United States - but even without the scenario of a major depression, the risk of a recession in combination with Moody's trading for high valuation multiples is posing a risk for the stock and makes lower stock prices in the coming quarters likely. Moody's is still a "Hold" for me. It is not a sell as I would seldom advice to bet against great companies with a wide economic moat - and Moody's is fitting that description perfectly.

Looking back at that statement, the “Hold” rating might have been correct to some degree, as Moody’s Corporation clearly underperformed the S&P 500 (18% total return for Moody’s vs. 32% return for the S&P 500). But I might have been a little too pessimistic in my last article.

In the last few weeks, Moody’s Corporation entered bear market territory again, losing more than 20% from its previous all-time high – in line with declines seen in several other software and financial services companies. In the following article I will explain why I think Moody’s Corporation might be at least fairly valued right now and why fears about GenAI might seem overblown.

Fiscal 2025 Results

We start by looking at the last reported results, and Moody’s Corporation performed well once again and beat analysts’ estimates for revenue as well as earnings per share – similar to previous quarters. Revenue in the fourth quarter increased from $1,672 million in Q4/24 to $1,889 million in Q4/25 – resulting in 13.0% year-over-year growth for the top line. Operating income increased 37.3% year-over-year from $561 million in the same quarter last year to $770 million in this quarter. Finally, diluted earnings per share increased even by 57.1% year-over-year from $2.17 in Q4/24 to $3.41 in Q4/25.

For the full year, results are similar, and revenue increased 8.9% year-over-year from $7,088 million in fiscal 2024 to $7,718 million in fiscal 2025. Operating income also increased from $2,875 million in the previous year to $3,351 million in fiscal 2025, resulting in 16.6% year-over-year growth. Diluted earnings per share also increased 21.4% year-over-year from $11.26 in fiscal 2024 to $13.67 in fiscal 2025. Free cash flow also increased slightly from $2,521 million in the previous year to $2,575 million in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 2.1% year-over-year growth.

Moody’s At Risk?

In the last few weeks, I wrote several articles about software and financial services companies that seem to be under pressure. When looking for reasons, it is mostly stated that GenAI is threatening these businesses or that investors are seeing the threat of GenAI and therefore the stocks declined in the last few months.

And while Moody’s Corporation clearly underperformed the S&P 500 in the last few months, it performed better than many other software or financial services stocks. When looking at the performance over the last few months, we see Moody’s Corporation performing the best among the stocks in the chart below, as Moody’s Corporation declined “only” 20% from its previous all-time high. And while looking at the performance over the last three years, Moody’s Corporation increased 46% and clearly outperformed the others.

When looking at the stock performance of Moody’s Corporation vs. its peers, we can conclude that investors seem to be more confident about Moody’s Corporation and assume the company will perform better than some of its peers. In the following sections I will take a closer look at the company to answer the question of whether Moody’s Corporation is less at risk and might be a good investment.

When looking at revenue growth in the last few years, we see that growth rates are fluctuating heavily over the past decades. However, I can’t see hints for growth rates slowing down due to threats from GenAI. The slowdown in 2024 and 2025 can be explained by the typical fluctuations.

Aside from revenue growth, we can also look at the margins Moody reported in the last few years. And we clearly see gross margin and operating margin contracting in the quarters around 2023, but especially operating margin improved again in the recent past and is above the long-term average again. In my opinion, margins are also not giving us any clue that Moody’s Corporation might be under pressure due to increased competition from GenAI.

Finally, we are looking at the return on invested capital the company is reporting. And while we see RoIC declining over the last 15 years, we also see the numbers improving again in the last few quarters, and it is difficult to see any signs of Moody’s Corporation being under pressure at this point.

Qualitative Analysis

Analyzing different metrics is certainly providing us with many insights, and so far, we can conclude that Moody’s Corporation is performing stably and there are not really signs of the business being under pressure. But numbers are certainly not telling us the full story, and it might also take some time before metrics like revenue growth will show serious troubles for the business.

My approach here will be similar to the analysis of Morningstar (MORN) and S&P Global (SPGI) in the recent past. I will split up the business into its different segments and look at the business models individually to determine whether the risk of GenAI might be justified or not.

We start by looking at Moody’s Investors Service, the segment providing credit ratings. In fiscal 2025, this segment generated $4,119 million in revenue (about 53% of total revenue) and grew 8.6% year-over-year. And although this segment has a rather low part of recurring revenue (34% in fiscal 2025, 37% in Q4/25), I see this segment protected extremely well from competition. Together with S&P Global, Moody's is dominating this market – a market that is built on trust and based on the brand name of these companies. Competitors might be able to offer good (or maybe even great) credit ratings by using great scoring systems and GenAI, but these companies will have to prove over a long time that their ratings are reliable. And it is not enough for a few companies or individuals to accept ratings from a competitor – the entire financial world, which is now focused on ratings from Moody’s or S&P Global, has to agree that the ratings of some new competitor can also be trusted and widely accepted.

When looking at the second segment, Moody’s Analytics, revenue in fiscal 2025 increased 9.2% year-over-year to $3,599 million. Revenue from this segment is generated mostly by subscriptions, and therefore recurring revenue was 97% (which is usually a good sign for investors). Nevertheless, this segment seems to be more at risk from GenAI applications. The first threat is one I also mentioned in the article about several other software companies or providers of financial data – the shift away from a subscription-based revenue model, which would lead to a lower rate of recurring revenue and lower levels of predictability.

Several articles (including one from the Financial Times and the IDC) are indicating that seat-based subscription models might be replaced by new models. IDC is writing:

Such a shift has profound implications for how SaaS is bought and sold. The traditional per-user, per-month licensing model becomes increasingly obsolete as digital labor replaces manual interaction. IDC predicts that by 2028, pure seat-based pricing will be obsolete, with 70% of software vendors refactoring their pricing strategies around new value metrics, such as consumption, outcomes, or organizational capability.

The second threat is the ability of GenAI to replace some of the services and offerings in a similar and cheaper way. And while some offerings like data visualization or basic research might be replaceable by using publicly available data, we must be very clear about the limitations of GenAI. Not only are competitors missing the models to provide high-quality forecasting or credit ratings. An even bigger problem is the proprietary data that Moody’s Corporation is possessing and competitors are missing, and without the data, the best GenAI applications are useless.

Finally, we should also not ignore that Moody’s Corporation can implement GenAI applications into its services and offerings or use GenAI to improve its ratings process. In my opinion, GenAI will rather widen the economic moat around Moody’s Corporation and does not really seem like a threat to the business.

Expecting Double-Digit Growth

At this point, we have reasons to be optimistic about Moody’s Corporation for the years to come. And this optimism is also visible when looking at management’s guidance for fiscal 2026. Revenue is expected to grow in the high-single digits once again, and diluted earnings per share are expected to be in a range of $15.00 to $15.60. Compared to fiscal 2025, this would result in 10% to 14% bottom line growth. Adjusted diluted earnings per share are expected to be in a range of $16.40 to $17.00 – resulting also in 10% to 14% bottom line growth. Finally, free cash flow is expected to be in a range of $2.8 billion to $3.0 billion.

For the long run, management is also optimistic about revenue growth in the long run. The company’s long-term revenue growth algorithm for its MIS segment is leading to 6% to 9% growth for the years to come. Growth will stem from economic expansion, the company’s value proposition, and developing capital markets.

As far as I can tell, Moody’s Corporation did not issue a similar long-term growth target for its MA segment, but I would also argue that Moody’s Analytics is able to grow in the mid-to-high single digits as well (and growth rates might be even more stable). Aside from top line growth, Moody’s Corporation might also increase its margins a little bit (but I would not be too optimistic here).

Additionally, the company will continue to buy back shares, and this will have an impact on bottom-line growth. During the last 12 months, the number of diluted shares outstanding declined 1.7% to 178.7 million. Since the late 1990s, the number of shares outstanding decreased about 2.4% annually.

According to the company’s guidance, Moody’s Corporation will spend about $2 billion on share buybacks in fiscal 2026, and when using the current stock price, it would be enough to repurchase about 4.5 million shares and lower the share count by 2.5%

Combining this, we can assume that Moody’s Corporation will be able to grow in the low double digits, and these growth rates are in line with analysts’ assumptions for the years to come.

Between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2030, analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 11.1% during these five years.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

In a final step, we must determine whether Moody’s Corporation is a good investment at this point or if the stock is still a bit too expensive to be a good investment. We can start by looking at the valuation multiples the stock is currently trading for. At the time of writing, Moody’s Corporation is trading for 31.3 times earnings as well as 29.9 times free cash flow.

When looking at the last ten years and comparing valuation multiples, we see the stock currently trading slightly below the long-term average for P/E ratio (36.7) and P/FCF ratio (33.4). Looking at these numbers, we can’t really call Moody’s Corporation a bargain, and it actually seems to be still expensive, but valuation multiples don’t account for growth, and therefore we use a discount cash flow calculation as well.

As always, we calculate with a discount rate of 10% (the annual return I like to achieve at least from any investment) and the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (178.7 million in Q4/25). The basis for our calculation can be a free cash flow of $2.9 billion, which is the midpoint of the company’s guidance (see above).

Looking at the free cash flow margin, it was 29.3% on average in the last one or two decades, and currently, free cash flow is a bit above average. Nevertheless, I think we can use the company’s guidance as a basis in our calculation, as margin might improve slightly over time – justify a margin above the long-term average.

In a first calculation, let’s be a little more cautious and assume only 9% growth for the next ten years, followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. This is leading to an intrinsic value of $377.18 for Moody's, and the stock would still be overvalued.

In a second calculation, we are a little more optimistic and assume 11% growth followed by 4% growth till perpetuity once again. Calculating with these assumptions leads to an intrinsic value of $431.13 for the stock, and we could call it fairly valued right now.

Wide Economic Moat

Before we come to our conclusion, one final hint that seems to be important. I am calculating with 4% growth till perpetuity, as the CFI recommends not calculating with a higher growth rate till perpetuity. And I certainly get the argument to be rather cautious about high growth rates in perpetuity, as we can’t know what will be in 20, 30, or 50 years from now, and if a company is outperforming the economic growth over several centuries, it will eventually overtop the economy at some point, which is impossible.

Keeping these reservations in mind, I would still argue for some businesses to be able to grow at a high pace for a longer time – and Moody’s Corporation is one company on a rather short and selected list (maybe a few hundred companies around the world) that might deserve a higher growth rate till perpetuity. Moody’s Corporation has a wide economic moat around its business, which becomes visible by looking at the (more or less) stable margins and the high return on invested capital, which was around 20% in the last 5 years.

And when, for example, assuming that Moody’s Corporation can grow by 6% in the 10 years following 2036 and once again 4% growth till perpetuity (from fiscal 2046), we get an intrinsic value around $500 for Moody’s Corporation, and the stock might be undervalued already.

Conclusion

At this point, we can make the argument that Moody’s Corporation is a cautious “Buy.” The stock not only seems to be at least fairly valued (maybe it is even slightly undervalued), but the wide economic moat around the business will protect the company and justify higher growth rates for a long time. Additionally, I see the threat from GenAI as rather limited – especially ratings are hardly affected at all.

data.title: War In Iran: Why Europe Could Be The Next Escalation Front
data.description:
Aerospace and defense companies are among the possible beneficiaries of the war in the Middle East. However, what we are seeing currently is that aerospace and defense stocks are actually trading lower since the war in Iran started. In this report, I discuss this somewhat counterintuitive development and highlight why tension in Europe can further increase, which theoretically would provide additional support for expanding defense budgets.

Aerospace And Defense Stocks Have Shown Weakness

Over a one-year period, SHLD and ITA have shown a return of 58.5% and 43.6%, respectively, which means that both ETFs outperformed the S&P 500 considerably. I attribute the outperformance of the Global X Defense Tech ETF relative to ITA to a combination of exposure to emerging defense tech such as Palantir Technologies and European defense contractors such as Rheinmetall, BAE Systems, and Leonardo. However, since the war started, both ETFs have lost value. ITA lost 8.4%, partially driven by higher commercial exposure and lower exposure to European defense stocks, which have performed better. SHLD lost around 3.2% of its value.

The top 10 holdings for both ETFs show that SHLD is much more defense-heavy, with over 75% of the top 10 holdings being near-pure defense, while this is 23%-28% for ITA. So, commercial exposure drives some underperformance due to macro-outlooks. Furthermore, ITA is a US pure play, while SHLD is more diversified with around 20% exposure to non-US names in the top 10, and the performance of those stocks has generally been better.

Why Even Aerospace And Defense Stocks Are Under Pressure

There are several reasons why aerospace and defense stocks are under pressure, but they mostly tie back to one common denominator, and that is the macro-outlook. Putting it simply, oil prices increased, and that may lead to reduced economic growth. Aerospace and defense is mostly seen as an industry that is immune or better resistant against economic weakness. That is not necessarily a wrong view. However, commercial aerospace is prone to economic weakness, and that does explain the weakness that ITA has put on display in the past weeks. Furthermore, we note that the budget environment is challenging in the sense that the Pentagon has an increased focus on transferring risk to the defense primes. If the macro-outlook weakens, that may happen in combination with inflation, and that means that labor and raw materials will also become more expensive. Coming out of the pandemic and into the energy crisis due to the war in Ukraine, many US defense contractors burned themselves on fixed-price contracts on which they were exposed to inflation and higher input costs. So, with inflation and recession fears once again emerging, the concern about profitability on contracts and risks of charges on defense contracts also resurfaces.

In Europe, I would say that fixed-price contract risks may be lower for defense contractors, but they're not fully absent. However, European countries have significantly increased defense budgets, and the money has to come from somewhere. If economic growth stalls, the question of where the money has to come from becomes bigger. So, some of the budget certainty has faded.

At the same time, we note that near-term growth for defense equipment and systems sales is not governed by incremental increases in demand but by the ability to supply. We see that missile defense and missile demand are increasing, but as I discussed in a prior report, this mostly drives mid-term opportunities through higher peak production and a longer time of elevated production rates. So, it doesn’t create the near-term capacity expansion required to benefit defense contractors now. Investors likely also have become increasingly aware of the fact that additional conflict is not significantly going to result in near-term opportunities.

As part of my stock screener on The Aerospace Forum, I am keeping track of the 10-year median development of EV/EBITDA multiples, and it shows that those 10-year multiples have significantly expanded. Currently the median is around 23.3x compared to a median of 22.6x since the start of 2025. So, the current multiple is still 3% above the median but more than 6% below the highs previously seen and definitely above valuation levels seen prior to the war in Ukraine, which were around 16x-18x.

We see some fluctuations in the trading multiples, which generally have coincided with peace talks with Russia stalling. The big difference now is that there is a possible macro shock involved. There was, of course, an energy price shock in 2022 as well when Russia invaded Ukraine, but what investors are now processing is the fact that additional conflict zones do not drive opportunities in the near-term valuation, and that means that there is more focus on the macro shock aspect of the current war.

Iran Conflict Could Trigger Escalation In Europe

While there is definitely pressure on aerospace and defense stocks and their valuation levels, we do note that the escalation in Iran may have a spillover to the war in Ukraine. In order to reduce the crude oil prices, the US has implemented a 30-day waiver for the ban of Russian oil for oil that is already at sea. At the current high oil prices, that would provide a lot of money for Russia to sustain the war in Ukraine, and that comes on top of the added benefit for oil that Russia was already exporting but now yields a higher dollar value per barrel. Russia has been forced to invent a low-cost war model, which it did through the utilization of drones. If oil dollars now flow back into the economy of Russia, then essentially we are seeing big dollar amounts flowing into a war economy that has learned to be cost-efficient. At the same time, Ukraine is also watching the current developments, and their response has been and likely will continue to be targeting Russia's energy infrastructure to prevent oil production, which, at current barrel prices, brings big dollars.

We also note that Iran’s targeting of Diego Garcia, around 2,500 miles away from Teheran, feeds the impression that Iran’s missile range is substantially larger than previously thought. The prior self-imposed limit on the missile range suggested that Iran’s missile could not really reach much further than Turkey and perhaps parts of Eastern Europe. This is shown as the red inner circle. The outer inner circle may be what Iran can truly reach, and that means that major parts of Europe are within reach.

While President Trump has been critical of Europe’s activity and support for the war in Iran and efforts to open up the Strait of Hormuz, we do note that in preparation for the war, fighter jets, tankers, and cargo airplanes were flown to the airports of Sofia and Crete. The primary airport of Bucharest, which is minutes away from where I live, hosts six tanker airplanes after the Romanian government initially provided clearance to station five airplanes. A sixth airplane joined on Sunday morning. At any given time, we are also seeing the air bridge active with EU NATO airspace being utilized by military cargo airplanes, and we are even seeing Canadian military airplanes flying more sorties within Europe. It potentially puts European countries in the crosshairs of Iranian missiles.

We do note that France has sent an aircraft carrier to the Mediterranean Sea accompanied by a Dutch naval vessel, and from east to west Europe, we are seeing military equipment being moved at a rapid pace.

NATO Alliance Comes Under Further Pressure

While NATO allies have opened up their airspaces and allowed US military equipment to use airspace and infrastructure, President Trump has been critical of the lack of commitment from allies to open up the Strait of Hormuz, “a simple military maneuver … with so little risk,” which runs contrary to the truth. Shaped as a bottleneck, zones like the Strait of Hormuz have, in the history of war, always been higher risk zones. Furthermore, NATO is a defensive alliance. With President Trump openly criticizing NATO allies, the tension within the alliance has only increased.

Europe Is Facing An Even Bigger Need For Defense Weapon System Recapitalization

While share prices of aerospace and defense names have come down, I do believe that the need for Europe to accelerate its push for security autonomy has only increased over the past weeks. That means that while macroeconomic uncertainty combined with the realization that near-term upside in the ability to supply is limited, the mid-to-long-term picture is that the need for autonomy and subsequently higher defense spending has found more support in the geopolitical spectrum. In the most bullish scenario, it would mean that growth will persist for a prolonged period. So, rather than economic growth being a limiting factor, I do believe that European countries will remain committed to increasing defense budgets and even raise debt levels if it is required to sustain higher defense budget levels.

I do believe that for US Aerospace and Defense companies, near-term upside through incremental increases in demand is also limited, but rising tensions in the Middle East as well as with Europe will require significantly higher defense budgets.

Conclusion: The World Is More Dangerous Today, Not Less

I believe that while aerospace and defense companies in the US and Europe have lost some of their value since the start of the war, driven by macro concerns and limited upside in financial results from the war (if any), the mid-to-long term tension levels are likely to remain elevated, and that would call for recommitting to higher defense budgets and perhaps even increasing defense budgets rather than toning down budgets. It still leaves potential cost inflation as a risk, but overall I do believe that the long-term investment case for aerospace and defense companies has substantially strengthened.

data.title: EchoStar: The SpaceX Deal May Already Be Priced In
data.description:
It is with some trepidation that I write this article, as I am keenly aware of the hype surrounding the SpaceX IPO and what it could mean for EchoStar (SATS). As such, I may be roasted in the comments. Let’s run the thesis anyway. The stock is up nearly 350% over the last year, partly reflecting optimism surrounding the IPO. The bad news is that the company still has a weak balance sheet and is still operating at a loss while trading at a massive premium to peers. It appears that the upside connected to the SpaceX deal may already be priced in.

The SpaceX Deal In a Nutshell

On September 8, 2025, news broke that EchoStar had struck a deal with SpaceX. Under the terms of the agreement, EchoStar would sell its AWS-4 and H-block spectrum licenses to SpaceX for $8.5 billion in cash and $8.5 billion in SpaceX stock. Investors understandably rejoiced, as the deal did several good things for long-suffering EchoStar.

First of all, it validates the value of the company’s spectrum assets, as somebody actually bought them, and to some degree also validates its strategy of focusing on satellite-to-device services. This is meaningful considering the broader industry shift towards hybrid satellite-terrestrial networks, and commercial cooperation in this area lowers investors’ perceived downside risk.

Secondly, it shores up the company’s balance sheet. According to Seeking Alpha figures, between December 2020 and December 2025, the company went from a roughly breakeven balance sheet position to a net debt position of a whopping $28 billion. This is not a case of a few bad years. This is an explosion of leverage, which likely has investors seriously wondering whether EchoStar can remain liquid with cash from operations in December 2025 of negative $99.4 million. Although $8.5 billion is a solid cash injection, the company will still have a net debt position. Much of the deleveraging that the market appears to be pricing in still depends on finalizing the transaction, further asset sales, and an increase in free cash flow.

Thirdly, and perhaps most importantly, the company gains exposure to SpaceX through its stock holdings. This means it has started trading as something of a proxy for the space exploration sector, and looking at multiples, it appears to have caused a rerating. In any case, the strategic pivot from a pure infrastructure operator to a more diversified company has excited many investors. The question is whether this actually reduces risk or just changes the risk profile.

An Interstellar Valuation

Due to the rally of almost 350% over the last year, the stock is now trading at some fairly lofty valuations. As earnings-based metrics are not meaningful due to the company’s operating losses, we’ll look at other metrics instead.

The first number that springs out is the EV/EBITDA value. Trading at nearly 42x times TTM EV/EBITDA means it’s more than 4 times as expensive on this metric as peers T-Mobile US (TMUS) at around 10.7x and Charter (CHTR) at around 6x. The forward EV/EBITDA looks a bit better, but at 32x, the SpaceX deal seems to be doing some awfully heavy lifting. It’s also important to look at the quality of earnings. Companies like T-Mobile US and Charter generate steady cash flow from large subscriber bases. EchoStar’s earnings are far less stable and still tied to a changing strategy and shrinking legacy businesses, making the multiple even harder to justify. Even if EBITDA improves from current depressed levels, declining subscriber counts, negative revenue growth, and very negative net income margins mean that investors may need to wait for reality to catch up to the valuation.

A vastly inflated EV/EBITDA multiple wouldn’t be so bad if the underlying growth and profitability were better than peers, but they’re not. Revenue growth of around -5% compares poorly to TMUS’ 8.5% and Charter, which is roughly flat. Telecom investors usually care more about stability than fast growth because these businesses require heavy investment. In that context, EchoStar’s declining revenue stands out. Even mature operators like Charter have managed to keep revenue roughly flat. Meanwhile, the 3-year EBITDA CAGR of -24% still reflects transition costs compared to TMUS’ 7.2% and Charter’s 1%.

Finally, margins. EchoStar’s gross profit margin doesn’t look too bad at around 26% versus TMUS’ 63% and Charter’s 55.3%. But, and this is a big but, the net income margin is currently at -96.6% versus TMUS’ 12.5% and Charter’s 9%, according to Seeking Alpha data tables. All in all, the stock appears to be trading as a space exploration company, but it’s not. I think it’s a telecommunications company that sold some spectrum and gained some stock exposure.

What Needs To Happen

Let’s assume the market is right. What is it actually betting on? First of all, a successful operating turnaround. Subscriber losses need to stabilize, service revenue needs to return to a positive number, EBITDA needs to stop declining, and investments in network buildout need to start actually making money instead of just inflating the company’s debt position. At 32x forward EV/EBITDA, this improvement is assumed.

Secondly, the balance sheet needs to improve. The recent cash injection helps but is not enough. What really needs to improve is the company’s free cash flow position, which may be achieved through further spectrum monetization and the operational improvements listed above.

Thirdly, investors need more clarity on what the stake in SpaceX actually does. After the November 2025 deal in which EchoStar sold another 15 MHz of AWS-3 spectrum licenses for $2.6 billion in SpaceX stock, the total transaction values are at around $11.1 billion. This alone accounts for about a third of the company’s current $34.4 billion market cap. It’s important to keep in mind here that transaction values do not necessarily translate into immediate liquid market value, as IPO structure, lockups, and timing will influence how and when EchoStar can actually make money from its exposure.

Until we know what the IPO valuation looks like, the rally looks like pure speculation. For EchoStar’s valuation to hold, the market needs to know, first of all, what its stake in SpaceX looks like in reality, and secondly, how this can be monetized. In the absence of real visibility and numbers, the space exploration proxy narrative may prove difficult to sustain, in my view.

Conclusion

EchoStar’s massive rally, in the absence of actual tangible improvement in operating metrics, seems driven largely by the SpaceX deal. While it does really reduce balance sheet risk, declining revenue and negative profitability cannot be ignored. At more than 30 times forward EV/EBITDA, the market is assuming that operating metrics will improve significantly, and perhaps more importantly, that the SpaceX stake will be monetized effectively. However, until more details emerge about the IPO and actual SpaceX exposure, or tangible progress is made on further spectrum monetization, the stock seems to be running on stardust.

data.title: Geopolitics Reshapes The Role Of Infrastructure Investments
data.description:
Geopolitical volatility is not only increasing investor demand for infrastructure assets; it is urgently reshaping where and how capital is deployed. As energy security, supply chain resilience, and digital sovereignty rise up policy agendas, infrastructure investments that expand capacity and relieve bottlenecks are becoming critical.

We believe infrastructure investments allow investors to prioritize three key objectives to support portfolios:

1. Harvest higher inflation

Geopolitical events create inflation through disruption to energy markets and supply chains. Infrastructure assets, which benefit from price rises and higher barriers to entry, can add positive inflation sensitivity into portfolios to protect real returns when inflation volatility episodes occur.

Many infrastructure revenues have the benefit of being linked, directly or indirectly, to inflation. These assets typically have high gross margin cost structures and can more efficiently harvest rising prices without the cost pressures faced by other industries. Regulatory regimes also often allow cost pass-through as well as concession agreements including inflation escalators and, in many cases, contracted assets have direct CPI adjustments to revenue.

However, there are differences in the inflation sensitivity of different infrastructure strategies, which make portfolio construction and manager selection important. For instance, operating assets have in-place physical networks and facilities which allow them to generate revenue without reliance on raw materials. For these assets, rising prices of construction inputs limits new capacity and the competitive positioning of the assets can strengthen. In contrast, the returns of greenfield development strategies can be highly sensitive to input costs rising during the development process and are more vulnerable to rising costs in supply chains.

2. Withstand growth shock

A defining feature of infrastructure is that it provides essential services to governments, communities and corporates. With the correct portfolio construction and investment selection, infrastructure assets can bring durable demand that’s resilient to market shifts. For example, investing in data centers focused on the needs of large, creditworthy corporates around data storage, cloud computing capacity and other use cases, are less likely to be impacted by cyclical economic or market gyrations.

Infrastructure assets such as utilities, social infrastructure and core digital networks can provide essential services that remain in demand across all economic environments. While broader economic activity may fluctuate, these services are rarely discretionary. In contrast, infrastructure sectors with more economic sensitivity face risks in this type of environment, such as airports and toll roads in the transportation sector.

3. Benefit from rising electricity prices

Demand for energy, especially electricity, has begun rising in the U.S. in recent years after two decades of stability. In other global markets, the rise in electricity demand has not begun; however, prices are now rising alongside the spike in natural gas prices from disruptions to Middle East supply. These trends and disruptions have had a direct impact on the strategic importance of energy security, which investment in energy efficiency, efficient power generation and renewable energy uniquely helps support.

Higher electricity prices can directly increase the return potential of renewable energy generation and efficient gas-fired power in the short-term. And over the longer-term, governments are prioritizing the energy security that comes from reliable supply, with a diversified generation mix and stronger grid stability. This policy priority underpins the already attractive economics of renewables, storage, transmission, and flexible generation assets in many markets. Where these investments were once environmental goals, today they are increasingly urgent nationally strategic priorities.

Many of these assets operate under long-term power purchase contracts or regulated return models, providing revenue visibility even during periods of price volatility. As demand for reliable and diversified power grows, infrastructure owners may benefit from structural capital deployment into the energy system. At the same time, investment selection is critical as political winds can blow unpredictably, from shrinking legislative support in the U.S. to windfall taxes in the UK and Europe.

U.S. demand for electricity rises

Total Electricity Consumption (indexed Jan 2011 = 100, trailing 12 months)

Investor implications

As geopolitical uncertainty spreads across global markets, private infrastructure may play an increasingly important role in investor portfolios. The asset class offers a differentiated set of return drivers anchored in essential services, long-term contractual frameworks, and structural demand that persists across economic cycles.

These characteristics help underpin infrastructure’s resilience. Unlike many traditional asset classes that are more closely tied to market sentiment or short-term economic fluctuations, infrastructure assets often derive value from stable, asset-level cash flows and services that remain critical to the functioning of economies.

The information, analyses and opinions set forth herein are intended to serve as general information only and should not be relied upon by any individual or entity as advice or recommendations specific to that individual entity. Anyone using this material should consult with their own attorney, accountant, financial or tax adviser or consultants on whom they rely for investment advice specific to their own circumstances.

Products and services described on this website are intended for United States residents only. Nothing contained in this material is intended to constitute legal, tax, securities, or investment advice, nor an opinion regarding the appropriateness of any investment, nor a solicitation of any type. The general information contained on this website should not be acted upon without obtaining specific legal, tax, and investment advice from a licensed professional. Persons outside the United States may find more information about products and services available within their jurisdictions by going to Russell Investments' Worldwide site.

Russell Investments is committed to ensuring digital accessibility for people with disabilities. We are continually improving the user experience for everyone, and applying the relevant accessibility standards.

Russell Investments' ownership is composed of a majority stake held by funds managed by TA Associates Management, L.P., with a significant minority stake held by funds managed by Reverence Capital Partners, L.P. Certain of Russell Investments' employees and Hamilton Lane Advisors, LLC also hold minority, non-controlling, ownership stakes.

Frank Russell Company is the owner of the Russell trademarks contained in this material and all trademark rights related to the Russell trademarks, which the members of the Russell Investments group of companies are permitted to use under license from Frank Russell Company. The members of the Russell Investments group of companies are not affiliated in any manner with Frank Russell Company or any entity operating under the "FTSE RUSSELL" brand.

© Russell Investments Group, LLC. 1995-2026. All rights reserved. This material is proprietary and may not be reproduced, transferred, or distributed in any form without prior written permission from Russell Investments. It is delivered on an "as is" basis without warranty.

Original Post

data.title: S&P Global: Macro Headwinds With Misunderstood AI Risks
data.description:
S&P Global (SPGI) is one of the most trusted and well-known financial data and analytics providers to the largest institutions globally, having a wide moat due to many different core segments like market intelligence, ratings, and indices that generate recurring fees from subscriptions. On top of a wide moat that is unlikely to be displaced by AI anytime soon, S&P also has an extreme amount of data that isn't accessible easily, as S&P does not allow LLM providers to train their foundational models on S&P's data. This is moving towards the commoditization of data, as S&P is also using this data to implement into its newly acquired AI companies for its own market intelligence platform, essentially building a new AI service that uses its own data, which is why it will be unique to other services in the market.

Despite being AI resistant, S&P's current AI potential remains unclear, as a shift from its traditional services to AI is a risk to its previously stable business model since AI implementation and success are not guaranteed. S&P is also likely to be affected by the recent fears in the market due to the ongoing conflict and the hawkish views from the Central Bank, as this will likely affect core segments, which might further affect guidance for 2026 that was noted as disappointing.

Due to a massive selloff from the start of February caused by continued fears of AI disruption, even though I believe that S&P won't be heavily affected by AI, the narrative of them getting affected has clearly impacted them over the last 2 months. Alongside many software names (the SaaS apocalypse), they are currently facing a narrative issue, a massive problem for long-term sentiment, which is what S&P has always brought to the table when it comes to its business. For investors believing in its long-term thesis, it continues to trade lower and cheaper than historical averages, opening up an opportunity and an attractive price point. I continue to believe that narrative plays a massive part in the stock, as I rate S&P Global at a 'Hold' during this chaos.

S&P Global's Moat

It is extremely hard for S&P to be truly disrupted with its wide moat, having years of reliability, which cannot be replaced easily since it requires regulation that isn't accessible. S&P Global Ratings have a NRSRO (nationally recognised statistical rating organisation), being one of the most preferred and respected ratings' data alongside Moody's.

This could be ratings for corporate bonds and sovereign debt, as a better rating would determine how much interest the borrower pays, since it directly points to how trustworthy a borrower is. This leads to a whole market that is dependent on these ratings to point to investors or lenders how safe the investment or ABS (asset backed security) is, as since it is heavily regulated and deeply rooted in trust for decades, the barriers for entry are extremely high and essentially a market that is impossible to penetrate, as S&P competes with other firms that also have the same moat as Moody's. Hence, the switching cost is high since a rating can only be approved and widely accepted by a small group, as AI will more likely assist with S&P rather than foundational LLMs issuing ratings due to an extreme difference in proprietary data.

This is also directly reflected in indices and its global intelligence platform. It has so much proprietary data that gives an in-depth insight into institutions that truly shows a better picture of a certain business. There are obviously extremely strict rules for its global intelligence platform; they aren't selling confidential internal company data, but financial intelligence, data analytics, and industry insights, as well as RatingsDirect, which is a source of past and current credit ratings and research. Since it is so deeply involved in credit ratings and a boatload of data that isn't easily accessible to the public, this segment isn't going away anytime soon, as data is increasingly getting commoditised with the push towards AI. This also directly reflects its energy/commodity insights segment, with the data focused on a different industry (energy) rather than just market intelligence.

Its indices segment also has a wide moat, being the benchmark for today's stock market, charging a fee for fund managers like Vanguard (VOO) to use S&P models. The behaviour of the market is often judged by the S&P 500's returns, which is the benchmark today for evaluating how the stock market is doing and to evaluate the market's reaction and behaviour to news and shocks. This is because the S&P 500 is the index to evaluate the volatility of companies, usually referred to as a beta of 1 and anything above a 1 is the difference (0.01 = 1%) to how volatile a stock is compared to the S&P 500. Though there has been a rise of ETFs over the last decade, the S&P 500 is the benchmark of the stock market, influencing trillions in notional volumes every year, as it will likely continue to be the main index for the foreseeable future.

From the Morgan Stanley Conference:

So that's Ratings, Indices, Platts commodities prices in our Energy division and the distribution of that proprietary content. That's our owned intellectual property. There's no other source in the world for that data.

Every customer that wants to access our data through these LLM providers has to have a contract with S&P Global on S&P Global paper, we control the pricing, the renewals, all of that. And so the disintermediation risk really isn't there

As seen above, this is the push towards commoditising its data and enhancing the experience through AI, as AI isn't a displacement like the narrative on the SaaS apocalypse, but rather a catalyst for S&P as user experience will be improved with newer tools, leading to higher premiums and potentially more vendor lock in as S&P would have AI layers on the proprietary data that they own, leading to better pricing like their push from price to value and more usable and segmented data for the user.

Though the AI narrative of software displacement is very much possible, S&P's main value isn't from the software it provides itself, but the reliability (ratings) it provides to institutions and the massive pile of data that it sits on. Though the narrative has affected S&P, which stems from early February, I really don't think that S&P's core business and value proposition will be affected heavily in any way, as I don't believe that credit ratings will originate and be validated from AI anytime soon, as the fears about S&P have been overestimated.

2025 Review and Looking Forward

Revenue grew 8% YoY with no particular segment showing weakness in 2025, with operating margins expanding across every segment as S&P Global continues to show an extremely well executed business.

Though Market Intelligence and Energy (the insight/data segments) continues to show slight weakness, being the largest segment in S&P Global. I think its involvement in AI is setting up for these segments to be growth machines, but it may still be too early to tell.

From the earnings call:

Subscription revenue, which constitutes roughly 85% of Market Intelligence, grew approximately 7%, both organically and as reported

For Market Intelligence, we expect to sustain solid organic constant currency growth in 2026 in the range of 5.5% to 7%

For Energy, we expect organic constant currency revenue growth of 5.5% to 7% in 2026.

The main reason why I believe the guidance for these segments looks quite weak is because of majorly subscription-based recurring revenue. Subscription-based revenue is extremely bullish for companies since it increases the predictability for future performance, but low single-digit growth tends to suggest slight weakness since it reflects growth from raising prices (inflation hovering mid 2%) rather than new customers, as new customer growth and client segment expansion are currently its execution priorities (slide 64). This is seen as the guided growth is trending down at the guided midpoints, which, I think, is bearish since its medium term financial targets over the next 3-5 years from its investor day presentation in 2025 aim for 6%-8%. However, I think this is a catalyst, as AI could bring an enhanced platform with S&P's new products, as it aims to shift from price to value, which, I think, is attractive despite its near-term negative sentiment right now (explored later in the AI segment).

Another near-term issue I believe could worsen is its billed issuance growth, as it is already guided to be around 1% to 5% growth in 2026. I also believe that its ratings segment may also be partially affected, which, alongside market intelligence and energy, has already guided for poorer numbers than its targets, with guidance for 2026 being 4% to 7% growth, while the medium-term guidance is 6% to 9%.

After a full year of interest rates being dovish, rate cuts have been constantly pushed back, as the probabilities of the Fed cutting rates are constantly getting pushed back to later meetings. With the surge of oil prices, central banks have turned hawkish, which is not bullish for S&P Global, as this suggests fewer bonds getting issued, which results in fewer fees, as its ratings system has nearly 50% of its revenue stemming from transactions like issuing new bonds. This is seen when interest rates started hiking in 2022, with the Q1 2022 earnings call highlighting a 12% decrease YoY in global bond issuance, 35% decline YoY in bank loan ratings, which followed on to Q1 2023, where global bond issuance fell 7% YoY. A high volatility index (VIX) and high fear in the fear and greed index also suggest that less risky bonds are getting bought (junk bond, in the fear and greed index page), as fears lead to fewer bonds getting issued overall. This point could also translate to weakness in the indices segment, which is the only segment that is currently guiding similar to its medium-term guidance from its investor day projections.

Though billed issuance spiked in February, March and April are extremely important to understand how weak billed issuance will actually be, as companies could be front-loading billed issuance out of fear that credit spreads will continue to spike. March's number could also spike since companies might be front-loading, and if so, April is the one to look out for. The current billed issuance growth average is 12.5%, which is significantly higher than the guided 1% to 5%, as the current situation on S&P's numbers is uncertain looking forward, despite a good start to the year.

As noted before the major conflict happened, many macro factors could influence global capital markets and commodity markets, as the Iran conflict has exactly led to volatility in the S&P, which tends to be the more reliable and secure stock. My personal predictions are that ratings, market intelligence, and billed issuance will show slight weakness, but indices and energy will do just fine. S&P 500 volume hasn't shown any signs of weakness yet, with no signs of major declines MoM, as I believe the current oil crisis is an opportunity. I believe that the Platts platform, focusing on commodity benchmark prices and price assessment, will be important across institutions, analysts, and governments during times of uncertainty to clarify fears and doubts about better insights and judgement.

The reason I haven't touched on the Mobility segment yet is because they excluded medium-term guidance in their investor day presentation, and investors will understand a better picture in Q2 when they hold their Mobility Investor Day to better understand its long-term vision and numbers, as Mobility is getting spun off. Mobility was guided for 7.5% to 9% growth in 2026, which are solid numbers for investors who are looking to hold S&P into this spinoff, as the future for this segment is bright with the evolving car industry from electric vehicles and full FSD vehicles in the future, as AI is getting implemented into cars and the whole driving experience.

S&P Global and AI

S&P is currently betting on AI with multiple acquisitions like With Intelligence and Enertel AI, as S&P believes that AI is a net tailwind, as mentioned in the most recent earnings call by management. As mentioned in the intro, S&P's proprietary data is not in foundational LLMs, as implementing AI agents into its services simply enhances customer experience and utility, as S&P could potentially monetise the value itself rather than just charging a flat subscription price (mentioned previously). This is seen with the recent push and partnership with Google, as it is expanding to Gemini Enterprise, as a way to offer an agentic experience that includes its proprietary data.

Valuation

S&P continues to trade at a premium to its sector, which, I believe, is completely justified with its wide moat that has taken over a century to build up. With the current narrative and fears, S&P has traded lower than its historical averages, with a potential upside of 33% if it just trades at its 5Y historical average on a non-GAAP P/E TTM basis.

The main question is what S&P's current situation in this crisis is, whether it is an opportunity for them or an uncertainty, as certain parts of the business will be affected, but certain parts of the business will be lifted, as a better picture will be painted in the next earnings. If investors believe that S&P will recover and the crisis is temporary, its price levels are extremely attractive compared to its historical valuations for a quality business with a wide moat.

S&P, compared to its close peer Moody's, isn't trading at an over- or undervaluation, as there is not a huge disparity in both valuations. Also seen above, Moody's and S&P's stock prices react virtually at the same rate, as whenever S&P trends up, Moody's trends up, same for when it is stagnant and down over the past year, as both firms are trading in exact situations where the stock is currently depressed due to poor narratives and uncertainty.

S&P's RSI has already bounced back from being in the deep oversold level (below 30 = oversold), as it currently sits at 40, which is just above the RSI oversold level. Though the current levels are not exactly oversold on the RSI, I still believe that S&P's current level is extremely attractive for investors who believe that S&P will bounce and recover from the current situation.

Final Thoughts

S&P is currently struggling with macro uncertainty, with central banks globally having hawkish mindsets as the probability for a US rate cut constantly gets pushed back, affecting core segments. It had already issued weak guidance in growth rates across the board (except indices) before the current escalations compared to its medium-term guidance, as S&P's stock price continues to reflect an uncertain market where no one really knows what is going to happen next. Though I like to see further clarification first for this brilliant company in the near term, its current levels are extremely attractive for investors who are betting on a recovery in sentiment, as I believe the AI displacement narrative on S&P is slightly ridiculous.

data.title: Flotek: Tailwinds Intact, But Valuation Keeps It A Hold (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

When I first wrote a detailed report on Flotek Industries (FTK), my thesis was mainly based on the turnaround and structural change in the revenue mix of the company. Since then, the stock price has grown by 54%. In my 3Q25 update, my major focus was on PWRtek and the Middle East contract's impact on financials. In that report, I forecasted that even after a 2-month rally, the stock could give 11% returns to investors. It grew almost 15% since my 3Q25 report.

Now, Flotek has reported its fourth-quarter results. The result supports the structural change of the revenue mix. It reconfirms the business is gradually moving away from its traditional, cyclical chemistry model toward a more stable, data-driven platform. The revenue growth looks strong, but the real story lies in its profitability mix.

At the same time, new opportunities like utilities infrastructure are emerging, even as geopolitical uncertainties continue to linger. With these updates, I have reiterated my estimates and valuation to check if there is any upside remaining.

Q4 Update

In Q4 and FY2025, with the help of the continued momentum in both businesses, Flotek's revenue grew strongly. As I have mentioned in the Q3FY25 update, the important highlight is not only growth but also a shift in the revenue composition. The shift was visible in the 9M performance, and the Q4FY25 result has also sustained that trend.

Currently, the Data Analytics segment's contribution to FY2025's revenue is about 12%, which is more than double that of last year's contribution. This transition shows that some part of the incremental revenue of the company is coming from the higher-quality, service-led offerings. The growth in this segment was mainly from the increasing adoption of real-time monitoring, regulatory compliance requirements, and the need for operational efficiency across the energy value chain.

Even though the contribution of the Data Analytics business is increasing, the Chemistry Technologies segment is dominating the revenue composition with about 88% contribution to FY2025's total revenue. On a full year basis, the overall revenue of this segment has surged by 19% YoY. Revenue from preparatory work for the Middle East contract and adoption of prescriptive chemistry management services were the primary reasons behind this growth. This suggests that both segments have solid growth engines.

The shift in revenue is not only impacting the revenue composition but also the gross margin composition. Because the Data Analytics business generates structurally superior gross margins compared to the Chemistry Technologies segment (legacy business), which can be seen in the gross margin composition. And as a result of the changing revenue mix, gross margin has expanded by 416 bps in FY2025 compared to FY2024. In Q4FY25, the gross margin contracted by 168 bps because, compared to last year's same period, Flotek earned a lower shortfall penalty, which has a 100% margin. However, if we look at the gross profit numbers, we will see a 24% YoY growth in Q4FY25.

The impact of revenue growth, change in revenue composition, and operating leverage can also be seen in full year EBITDA margin, as it grew by 370 bps. However, in 4Q25, operating expenses have eaten slightly higher part of sales, and shortfall penalties also declined compared to the year ago, which contracted EBITDAM slightly.

The balance sheet of the company looks stable with moderate leverage. Currently, the debt-to-equity ratio stands at 0.35x, and it's low because of the collapsed equity due to piled-up losses in past financial years.

In FY2025, the net income margin has also expanded substantially because of the change in revenue mix. However, in 4Q25, the net income margin collapsed significantly. The good news is that it was not because of any problem in business, but rather because of the higher effective tax rate. Earlier, the company had tax benefits, and now those benefits are being normalised. In Q4FY25, the effective tax rate was 35%, which was abnormally higher than the prior year's tax rate of approximately 7%. The management has said that going forward, it can stabilise at about 21%.

So, to sum up the financials, I think it's very evident that the business is not just growing, but also changing structurally, which is improving the quality and sustainability of its earnings. Flotek is slowly stepping away from its traditional volume-driven chemistry business and moving towards a higher value service business.

The changing revenue and margin composition of Flotek is making its business more stable and predictable. Because the chemistry business remains inherently cyclical, tied to rig activity, customer spending, and broader industry conditions, while the data analytics generates stable, recurring revenue.

What will happen next?

In my last report on Flotek, I highlighted three emerging growth engines of the company. Those growth engines are early monetisation of PWRtek, rising adoption of Data Analytics services, and potential upside from the Middle East client. In this quarter, we have some updates about all previously mentioned growth engines, and some new growth drivers have become visible.

Now, let's start with PWRtek. In FY2025, PWRtek earned revenue of about $15.8 million. In my Q3 update, I mentioned that this business can earn $27 million in annual revenue for the next five years. This revenue growth is again confirmed by management in the latest conference call. Therefore, I am not making any changes in the estimates for this growth engine.

The new growth engine, which can contribute to the Data Analytics business, is the company's entry into the utilities and infrastructure contract. For this, the company will leverage the PWRtek platform to facilitate operations and real-time monitoring to maximise system performance and minimise downtime. Flotek will install up to 50 megawatts of power generation equipment, including advanced gas distribution and smart conditioning systems. The management has said that they are expecting $1 million in revenue per MW. But this revenue is expected to ramp up gradually in phases. Currently the time span of the contract is 6 months, and it is expected that execution will start in Q2FY26. The management has also highlighted in the conference call that there is a high possibility that the contract will get an extension.

Currently, we only know that the contract's initial timeframe is 6 months, and it is most likely to get an extension. The management has commented that this project is going to be installed in two phases. The first phase deployment will be executed in the existing infrastructure of the client, while the second will be executed in a new infrastructure that has not yet been built.

I think this project won't reach its full potential in a 6-month timeframe and will get an extension because building new infrastructure will take time. Given the phased deployment, mid-year start, and lack of clarity on final capacity, I conservatively assume partial utilization in FY2026, with full ramp-up likely only by FY2027. We do not know exact figures, which is why I am assuming 50% capacity utilization in FY2026.

Despite the Middle East geopolitical tension, management highlighted that operations in the Middle East are tracking toward the upper end of expectations. Demand is strong, execution is on track, and overall operations are running smoothly for now.

The ongoing geopolitical tensions are starting to create some friction, especially around shipping routes. Because of that, Flotek has to reroute or use alternative methods, which increases logistic costs. So, while revenue and activity remain intact, margins could take a bit of a hit in the near term. Therefore, I am not changing my revenue estimates from the Middle East project for now, but I will stay cautious.

Another impact of the geopolitical tension is that it can increase the rig counts in the USA, which will eventually increase the frac activity because global oil supply constraints could arise after a war. This can be beneficial for both business segments of Flotek. However, I believe it's too soon to integrate the impact into the estimates because rising oil prices due to supply constraints can also affect the demand. We will get a clear picture once the geopolitical tension starts easing up. Also, the U.S. rig count continues to hover around levels seen at the time of the Q3 article.

Overall, there is no major change in the revenue estimates of both segments as compared to the Q3 article because, more or less, the conditions for both segments are the same. The core thesis is still intact. Only the addition of the revenue from the new utility infrastructure contract is needed. I was worried about the geopolitical situation in the Middle East, as it was one of the major growth drivers. However, the management has confirmed that the demand is still intact for them.

In Q3FY25, the management EBITDAM guidance for FY2026 was 16% - 17.8%. I assumed 17% EBITDAM for the EBITDA calculation of FY2026. However, I think it's better to keep it at the lower end because the management has said that Flotek's operational cost can increase in the near term because of logistics issues. With 17% EBITDAM, the company's EBITDA in FY2026 could be about $51 million.

Valuation

To calculate the fair value of Flotek, I have included peers suitable for both segments. These peers have been consistently included in my relative valuation of Flotek since my first article on the company. Currently, the peer average EV/EBITDA is 12.5x.

Even though there is a possibility that after the war we can see an increase in US rig counts, I would stay cautious because the Middle East contract is one of the major growth engines for Flotek in future. Also, the rising oil prices can negatively impact the global demand. So, I am assuming that the company will trade at the peer average EV/EBITDA for a while. With $51 million EBITDA and an EV/EBITDA of 12.5x, we arrive at an enterprise value of about $631.5 million. After adjusting for the net debt of $45.9 million, we will get a market cap of $586 million, which implies about 5% downside from its current market cap of $614 million.

Risks

Even though the Middle East's geopolitical tension can boost the US rig counts, which is beneficial for both segments, the Middle East contract, according to my thesis, which can be one of the major revenue contributors of Flotek in the coming years, can suffer negatively. Currently, the geopolitical tension is only impacting Flotek's logistic cost, but any significant impact on revenue can substantially affect the valuation of the company.

The utilities contract, which is a new addition to the growth engines of Flotek, is very promising, but it carries an execution and visibility risk. Because the phase 2 execution depends on the construction of new infrastructure, which can take more than the announced timeframe of the contract. If the contract does not get extended or Flotek delays the execution, it can impact the revenue of the future financial years.

Conclusion

Flotek's story is no longer just about recovery. It's about transition. Even though the current valuation appears stretched, management remains confident about the Middle East contract despite ongoing geopolitical tensions.

In fact, these tensions could indirectly support US rig activity, creating an additional tailwind. There is also a possibility that rising oil prices due to supply constraints that war will cause can impact the global oil demand.

Importantly, no immediate headwinds are visible that could materially disrupt the company's valuation. The core thesis remains intact with growth holding steady and a clear shift in revenue mix and profitability toward higher quality, service-led segments. Currently, valuation looks expensive, but the structural transformation and strong growth engines justify maintaining a hold stance for now.

data.title: Eurozone Bank Lending Growth Was Steady Before The War
data.description:
By Bert Colijn, Chief Economist, Netherlands

In February, monetary developments were relatively steady, according to the ECB. Bank lending to non-financial corporates ticked up, from 2.8% annual growth in January to 2.9% in February. For households, year-on-year growth remained at 3%. 2025 saw an acceleration in bank lending growth, but this has slowed towards the end of the year, and currently it looks like bank lending growth has plateaued a bit around the 3% level.

But the February data is backwards-looking. Since then, the war in the Middle East has upended the global economy. Bank lending conditions have changed, even though the conflict only started a month ago. With yields having increased significantly across the curve, this has made financial conditions less attractive. And with concerns about the economic impact of the conflict rising, borrowing for investment purposes could be dampened by the conflict.

At the same time, though, supply chain disruptions could increase the need for working capital, which could increase borrowing temporarily, as we saw in 2022. So, while the conditions for investment are weakening for now, lending could see a temporary acceleration. After the steady environment that today’s data release from the ECB depicts, conditions have clearly been changing thanks to the war. For eurozone investment, risks to the downside have clearly become more prominent.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: French Businesses And Consumers Are Becoming More Pessimistic
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier, Senior Economist, France and Switzerland

Sentiment is deteriorating

The business climate in France remained stable in March, at 97, but has now been below its long‑term average for almost two years. Responses were collected by INSEE between 26 February and 23 March. However, the vast majority of firms responded before mid‑March, at a time when the repercussions of the conflict in the Middle East were still limited compared with the current situation. Nearly one-fifth of responses date from before the outbreak of the conflict.

As expected, the business climate deteriorated sharply in industry, losing three points over the month. Past production, production expectations, and order books are all down. This deterioration is mainly driven by the “other transport equipment” sector, which had been one of the main engines of French production in recent months but is now markedly more pessimistic. At the same time, selling price expectations in the industry rose sharply over the month.

Sentiment remains very depressed in services due to a more negative assessment of past activity. By contrast, the business climate edged up slightly in retail trade and construction. The employment climate improved by one point but remains well below its long‑term average.

Household confidence also declined in March, falling by two points over the month. Although only half of survey responses were collected after the outbreak of the conflict in the Middle East, households are anticipating a very sharp rise in prices in the coming months. The corresponding balance of opinion jumped by 28 points and reached its highest level since September 2022. Households are also markedly more pessimistic about the future evolution of their personal financial situation.

Overall, the data published today confirm that French economic prospects have deteriorated significantly since the outbreak of the conflict in the Middle East. Given the survey collection timetable, the impact of the most recent developments is not yet fully reflected in the March data. If the conflict does not ease in the coming days, a much sharper deterioration in sentiment can be expected in April.

French inflation will rise, but less than elsewhere in Europe

The impact of the situation in the Middle East on the French economy will largely depend on the duration and intensity of the conflict. One consequence already appears certain, however: inflation will be higher than previously expected. At the start of 2026, our forecasts pointed to average inflation of 1.3% for the harmonised consumer price index in France. We now expect inflation to be close to 2% over the year as a whole, with a risk of further upward revisions.

That said, this revision remains more limited than in other European countries. France’s inflation starting point is lower than in most other euro area countries. In addition, the French economy is characterised by a large share of administered or centrally negotiated prices, notably in large‑scale retail and energy. This means that increases in commodity prices are transmitted more slowly to consumer prices than elsewhere. Finally, the economic environment prior to the conflict was not particularly dynamic, which reduces the risk of a rapid inflationary spiral.

The labour market also remains fragile. As shown by the employment climate, hiring intentions are weak and net job creation has been negative for several quarters. Wage negotiations for this year do not incorporate this new inflationary environment. As a result, wage growth should remain moderate, helping to contain price increases, particularly in services.
